message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Introduction</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Epidemiological and laboratory research seems to suggest that smoking and perhaps nicotine alone could reduce the severity of COVID-19. Likewise, there is some evidence that inhaled corticosteroids could also reduce its severity, opening the possibility that nicotine and inhaled steroids could be used as treatments.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>In this prospective cohort study, we will link English general practice records from the QResearch database to Public Health England’s database of SARS-CoV-2 positive tests, Hospital Episode Statistics, admission to intensive care units, and death from COVID-19 to identify our outcomes: hospitalisation, ICU admission, and death due to COVID. Using Cox regression, we will perform sequential adjustment for potential confounders identified by separate directed acyclic graphs to:</jats:p>
                  <jats:list list-type="order">
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Assess the association between smoking and COVID-19 disease severity, and how that changes on adjustment for smoking-related comorbidity.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>More closely characterise the association between smoking and severe COVID-19 disease by assessing whether the association is modified by age (as a proxy of length of smoking), gender, ethnic group, and whether people have asthma or COPD.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Assess for evidence of a dose-response relation between smoking intensity and disease severity, which would help create a case for causality.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Examine the association between former smokers who are using NRT or are vaping and disease severity.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Examine whether pre-existing respiratory disease is associated with severe COVID-19 infection.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Assess whether the association between chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) and asthma and COVID-19 disease severity is modified by age, gender, ethnicity, and smoking status.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Assess whether the use of inhaled corticosteroids is associated with severity of COVID-19 disease.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>To assess whether the association between use of inhaled corticosteroids and severity of COVID-19 disease is modified by the number of other airways medications used (as a proxy for severity of condition) and whether people have asthma or COPD.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>This representative population sample will, to our knowledge, present the first comprehensive examination of the association between smoking, nicotine use without smoking, respiratory disease, and severity of COVID-19. We will undertake several sensitivity analyses to examine the potential for bias in these associations.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Associations between COVID-19 infection, tobacco smoking and nicotine use, common respiratory conditions and inhaled corticosteroids: a prospective QResearch-Case Mix Programme data linkage study January-May 2020
